author: @bodymindresiliencechannel
text: This is such a great resource - thankyou so much.  I've been thinking how this would fit on my roof, and whilst the Darwin collector works well, it results in a stack that's pretty high.  Your demo looked like the waters turbine is actually a centrifugal fan in reverse...  Another possibility is to use a hyperbolic cone in the middle of the original generator you made 2 years ago, so that it organises the air inside the fan as it moves out through the top.  I suspect this would also change the aerodynamics on the transition of air entering the fan from the side, and increase the efficiency without the need for a collection tower...??  Of course, there will be different things happening at different wind speed ranges.  Given that the vwt doesn't self-regulate, what happens in a 70mph wind gust?

author: @GoogleMapsVids-Asia
text: I was mechanic for the airlines all my life. I had given an idea to the county to put giant baffles at end run way. Airplanes generally sit throttling they’re engines just before take off. During flight a 747 engine creates 240.000 pounds of thrust in flight at take off I’m sure more. Imagine. Directing that trust to generators. My want was to send energy under the ramp for de- icing. Also to power the airport. Most times there a airplane running all the time.  I also wanted this to be a way to keep ice off planes as it sits. About 10 tp 30 min before take off. Many times up to 5 to 10 planes sit there wasting valuable fuel. This freak power could be used for treating salt water. Even a back up power system for the whole airport or city.

author: @Quasime42
text:
As we move away from solid fuel fires we have more domestic chimneys with no purpose. 
Turn that upside down  put a cap on the chimney ( like a malt house malting room cone) and  close the hearth but use a pipe to extend the chimney trough the wall to the outside of the house. 
Using the draw from the chimney even on a calm day you should get some draw. On a windy day you can get a Dyson effect going to draw a much bigger volume of air through. 
It might not give the same force as a simple turbine perpendicular to the wind flow, but it should be more constant trickle feed of charge? 

Might make it easier to store the energy or better if it's heating water for underfloor heating or similar

author: @justtinkering6713
text: In "lift type" turbines especially vawt, when considering lift, you have to take into account that following blades/wings are going through dirty or turbulent air, and have less lift because of it, when used in the normal way. But putting the air into it the way you did avoids the dirty air factor, i think.

author: @robertosullivan1822
text: Been working around turbines for the last 13 years and have come across a lot of guys trying to make power using VAWT. The big issue is the drag the carry in high winds. The wind can't escape quickly enough and this drag can self destruct after a period of time. This is the biggest issue that has to overcome with any of these turbines.

replies.text: @robertosullivan1822 Hey Robert, thanks for your comment. I myself am designing something for commercial applications. Do you have any improvements or suggestions for designing around drag?

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
4:23 The ONLY thing better is if you can INCLUDE...

"FORCED" convection [such as a chimney or other way to enhance the worthwhile properties of your ambient 'working fluid' [air, of course] so that you get MULTIPLE sources of motivation across the turbine, without adding anything to the factual turbine its self.

One really radical [and for me at the moment, totally inapplicable] element is if you have some location you control [a basement, a shed covered with lovely greenery, or just basically any place where air will cool in the summer and 'pool up'] OR, if you have some form of method to take advantage of the sunlight (I admit anybody who does not live in places like where I live are BOUND to have SOME forms of restrictive, or restricting, or legally prohibitive laws to deal with, as I live in a place where those kinds of laws simply do not exist) to create a thermal up-draft, anything at all to modify the functional pressure differential across bodies of fluids <in this case gases> in motion, such that they can then, while seeking to self-equalize [a simpler word for the ill fated, yet indispensable word "ENTROPY" ] will then WORK HARDER, in such a manner (such as higher wind speeds inside your system, as compared to outside of it) that helps increase said driving fluidic forces, so that more of said air/wind/water [thus they insist we all use the term 'fluid' in engineering class] can impinge upon your intended turbine, and, as a result, increase the potential of more harvestable power to you in the end.

author: @ErrantProjectile
text: To avoid lowering the wind speed mount the turbine on the horizontal, use a funnel to feed the air in, shroud the turbine with a body that flares out just beyond the circumference of the funnel.Mount it vertically on a pivoting post and build a power slip rings transfer point. The wind will force the turbine to point into the oncoming air by deflection on the rear body, the funnel will force the air into turbine and by having the air flare out on the backside, you also get a low pressure vacuum effect.  As well the turbine will be provided cooling by the air flowing around it.  Don't forget the hyperbolic cone!  I literally just pulled a turbine from a RV unit 15 minutes before seeing this video and debating on trashing it (coils are fine, a couple housing bolts broke is all)! Just have to convert it to DC output!  Now I have to go get some fiberglass and styrofoam, and neodymium magnets.  Project for the week! 😊

author: @user-td5th1lz7e
text: Can somebody please help me I am building a gas powered permanent magnet 12 SI alternator generator for 12 V system. How many magnets do you need to use? Is there too little or too many

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it doesn't matter very much mate - emf=Blv sine theta. So, speed of rotation and length of wire are going to impact a lot as well as strength of magnetic field - if your field isn't strong enough - spin it faster or stuff more wire in to compensate

replies.author: @user-td5th1lz7e
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering thanks for the reply I have a 12 SI 87 Ap 12V I bought 10 n 52 permanent magnets 2 inches by half inch by 1-inch. I heard people mention the number of windings versus how many magnets I did not know to be exact I didn’t want to make a mistake it’s a lot of work to make a rotor. I didn’t want to have to do it twice

author: @uniphi100
text: Hi Rob. Thanks for mentioning my turbine. Couple thoughts. Yes. this will work but it does add complexity. Everything's a trade off right? I originally designed the turbine as a simple principle to just make a point about efficiency. Betz ignores the idea of increasing velocity first for free (like a ridge). In it's simplest form, actually pretty simple to operate as a HAWT because it's theoretically so much smaller. Also works on an outside AC vertically. Btw, a squirrel fan is not optimum. It would be like using a propeller on a wind turbine. Squirrels still work though.

author: @dr.froghopper6711
text: I’ve learned more practical engineering from this channel in two years than I got in university in6 years.

replies.author: @archibaldvonkranski8881
replies.text: What were you studying mate? I'm starting a bachelor's degree in electrical engineering but I'm a little unsure where it leads. I'm more interested in power grids and mass generation ie solar, hydro and wind. Got any tips?😎🙏

replies.author: @topspeed250k5
replies.text:
​@archibaldvonkranski8881 I know you weren't asking me, but I did Engineering then changed fields, now pondering how my life would have been, had I stuck with Aero Eng 😊
I'd say "stay flexible" and drive your career.  I've found that it doesn't matter in science, whatever your qualification, the real learning happens after you leave school/uni and God, I wish the internet and channels like this and Tech Ingredients, had been around when I was studying.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: wow - cheers mate that was a kind thing to say - all the best

replies.author: @evanbarnes9984
replies.text: ​​@ThinkingandTinkering EE is a really solid degree that will open a bunch of doors, even if you want to change to something more mechanical or something later. All fields of engineering are built on the same foundation of math and physics, after all! But hell, just stay flexible and try a lot of weird new things! I got my bachelor's in psychology in 2012, became a shop and math teacher, and now I'm a product designer at an educational toy company, so who knows where you'll wind up! Also if you're interested in large scale power generation, I wouldn't be surprised if the path into that was through internships or entry level jobs with companies in that field toward the end of your bachelor's degree. Or maybe it's by developing a relationship with a mentoring professor! The biggest resource in university is the people you have access to, and the knowledge, experience, and opportunities they can offer you, so really make use of that.

author: @allanjacques1738
text: Isn't there greater efficiency when the wind strikes the squirrel cage at any  right angle? I would like to see you  invert one atop the other with a fixed coil between the two counter-rotating  magnet rimmed  Squirrel cages or Darius , or Ugrinsky etc. You have demonstrated that the counter rotating perimeter magnets produce greater output and that multidirectional wind capture is preferred. A counterrotating "hub generator"  could be mated with various  VWAT blade types and configured in stacks or  side by side in a "wind wall"

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, when you showed that downward draft of air would turn the turbine, I immediately thought of your deflector. Now take the exhaust and pass it across the outside AC unit. It will blow air across the coils and make the AC more efficient as it cools, especially on hot days.

author: @keithianlocke
text: Surely the squirrel cage spins regardless of wind direction anyway. Wouldn't this just add a layer of complexity, and possibly reduce efficiency?

@Luc-1991 if I listened correctly, a shroud to surround the waters turbine?
So what's gonna happen to the air?.. won't a shroud create a pressure bottleneck.

replies.author: @WilliamTythas
replies.text: the tower is to take advantage of higher wind speeds higher in the air ... this keeps the moving parts on the ground and not 150ft in the air for servicing .. the shroud around the fan would be vented of course and would be there so there weren't conflicting airflows from ground level wind

replies.author: @keithianlocke
replies.text:
@WilliamTythas the issue I see isn't with finding new tech to spin a generator. There are numerous efficient, and less efficient devices, driven by various natural energy sources.
The challenge is still the generator, and the size of present designs required to generate a decent amount. The size and capacity of the generator will always determine the required size of input.

author: @replikvltyoutube3727
text:
I wonder if similar thing could be utilized say on bicycle to turn drag into useful work...
That is an initiation to some idea I have about air powered transport.
TL;DR is Veritasium's "faster than wind" air powered (essentially bicycle) vehicle, however it used directional fan. My idea is how such transport could fair with a omnidirectional "air engine" added to it?

author: @yaka2490
text: imo rob you might yield better efficiency if you also put a converging nozzle from the the darwin collector exit point and then in the throat have a venturie or 3 to amplify the V of the air even more. This would also create a vaccum in the throat area which will again yield even more results and act as a positive feedback ramp mate... easy to test the input output V with anni as well.  the diverging nozzel on the base of the squirrel cage is also and great addition nice.... your MOI is very low due to the maglev so i suspect this system should show excellent results.

replies.author: @themeek351

replies.author: @DaremoKamen
replies.text: I had a similar idea, the Darwin turbine set to blow upward so that it adds to the chimney effect of a long pipe that pulls air through the sides of a squirrel cage and up from the center.  Again you would have the working parts low where they are easy to work on.  I think that having the Darwin directed downward it will lose efficiency since it has to fight the chimney effect rather than add to it.  I welcome experimental evidence to show whether that is correct or not.

author: @screen-protector
text:
That's all cool :). But, if we're talking some real life solution, we'd have to go for 1kWh generation at least. When knowing that 1.5mm diameter of enameled copper wire can carry 10A, or 2.5mm can carry 20A, we'd need at least 50-100meters or the wire. And that is a lot ;). If we're talking a smaller diameter, will be even longer. 

So, should we do several generators in parallel setup? Like a windwall? If we can do at least 5 of them, we're talking now only 200W/section. I'd say it's more reasonable, than 1kW. 

I can't grasp the size needed in order to build this 1kWh in my head. I think more people would like to know it as well. Maybe someone is smart enough to answer this question. I'm talking wind turbine only.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you already know the answers mate - they already build 1kW turbines and everything that governs the size of them governs the size of this - the only thing that will change it is efficiency so size will change in roughly the same percentage gain or drop in efficiency that this has

author: @douglascox9996
text: If you want to upscale the design and lack a 3-D printer or a ready-made squirrel cage, it should be possible to re-use Venetian blind slats as rotor blades. Thank you for sharing. 5:42

author: @Xero1of1
text: Okay. I like the idea. I have a thought though. I think you need to make your squirrel cage thinner... so instead of having the big blades like the one you have, you increase the radius of circle it's built upon and shorten the fan blades to an inch or two. The wider diameter will allow a larger flow of air to escape, and the shorter fans (assuming the pipe you connect this to is just as wide, or if of a smaller diameter, you close the extra diameter of the squirrel cage top off) will cause it to spin faster because you have a larger volume of air flowing through a smaller area. Less diffusion of air, as it were. You should be able to 3d print a cone for inside the squirrel cage to help direct the airflow outwards...

replies.author: @Xero1of1
replies.text: oddjobbob I think what he plans to do is put his wind catcher up on a hollow pipe and put it up in the air so it will direct the air downwards through the pipe, and then have this squirrel cage at the bottom to generate off of the captured wind. If that's the case, then since the squirrel cage will be at the bottom, there will be no interference with the wind at the top and you can make it as big or small as you want.
